title: Chapter 1: The Divorce Papers
content: The slap of paper on marble cut through the banquet hall like a blade.
Ren Zhou opened his eyes—and the last thing he remembered was the sky burning.

Not this ceiling. Not the chandelier's thousand crystals throwing fractured light across three hundred guests in tailored suits and silk gowns. Not the string quartet playing Vivaldi while waiters circulated with champagne.
Not the woman across from him, sliding divorce papers across the table with the surgical precision of someone removing a tumor.
"Sign it."

Liang Xue's voice could have frozen mercury. At twenty-four, she was the Ice Queen of Crystal City's first family—porcelain skin, obsidian hair pinned in a severe knot, eyes the color of a winter sea. Beautiful the way a glacier was beautiful: cold, remote, and capable of crushing anything in its path.
Ren's hand twitched.
Not toward the pen. Toward her throat.

The instinct hit him like a freight train—primal, absolute, the muscle memory of a man who had killed gods. His fingers curled, and in that instant he could see it: the precise angle to snap her neck before any of the three hundred guests could scream. Then the guards. Then—
A crimson interface exploded across his vision.
⚠️ **[SALVATION SYSTEM — CRITICAL ALERT]** ⚠️

`CAUSAL BACKDOOR STATUS: ACTIVE`
`MARRIAGE BOND: LIANG XUE ↔ REN ZHOU`
`FIREWALL INTEGRITY: 100%`

`WARNING: Dissolution of marriage will revoke spousal recognition.`
`WARNING: Causal firewall collapse imminent.`
`WARNING: Dimensional portal premature activation probability: 100%.`
`COUNTDOWN TO APOCALYPSE UPON DIVORCE: 3 SECONDS`
`RECOMMENDATION: Do NOT sign. Do NOT separate. Do NOT die.`
The red text pulsed like a heartbeat. And beneath it, smaller, almost gentle:
`— Shen`
Ren's hand stopped three inches from Liang Xue's throat.

His fingers uncurled, one by one, like a man defusing a bomb inside his own body. The War God in him screamed to annihilate everything in this room. The man who'd watched Earth die whispered: *No. Not again.*
He remembered now. All of it.

The six-year war against the Abyss. The dimensional gates tearing open across every continent. Cities swallowed. Armies erased. The last stand in the Himalayas, where seven billion had become seven thousand, and seven thousand had become seven, and seven had become—
Him. The last one. The War God who'd held the line while reality collapsed around him.
And Shen Wei, who'd driven a blade through her own chest to stitch the last remnant of human will into a system that could send one soul backward through time.
*One chance,* she'd said, blood on her lips and light in her eyes. *One chance to end this before it begins.*
He'd failed once. Earth was ash and screaming silence in his wake.
Now he was here—three years before the gates. Three years before the Patriarch of the Liang family completed the dimensional array and tore a hole in reality big enough for the Abyss to pour through.
And the only thing standing between humanity and extinction was a marriage that everyone in this room wanted destroyed.
Ren looked at the divorce papers.
He looked at Liang Xue.
He looked at his own hands—smooth, unscarred, twenty-six years old and utterly, pathetically mortal.
Then he smiled.
It was the kind of smile that belonged on a wolf watching trappers set their snares. The kind of smile that made the string quartet's first violin miss a note.
"You know what, Xue?" He picked up the pen—slowly, deliberately, the way a man reaches for a weapon. Liang Xue's eyes narrowed, expecting capitulation.
Ren set the pen down on the table, capped.
"I think our marriage can be saved."
The silence that followed could have swallowed the banquet hall whole.
Liang Xue's expression didn't change. But something flickered behind her eyes—not anger, not confusion. Something sharper. Wariness.

Beside her, Liang Feng—her younger brother, Ren's brother-in-law, and the man who would one day become a vessel for the Abyss—laughed. The sound was too loud, too sharp, and it bounced off the marble walls like shattered glass.

"You? Save the marriage?" Feng grabbed a wine glass from a passing waiter. "The Zhou family's broke. You have no money, no power, no *value*. You're a parasite, Ren. A leech my sister was stupid enough to marry."
"Feng." Liang Xue's single word cut through his laughter like a knife through silk.
Feng's jaw tightened, but he fell silent.

Ren watched the exchange with the eyes of a man who had seen civilizations end. Feng's hostility was expected. The spore growing in his chest—Ren couldn't see it yet, but he *knew* it was there. In the previous timeline, Liang Feng had been the first to fall. Stage 5 infection within two years. He'd opened a secondary gate in the family compound while the main portal drew everyone's attention.
Eleven million people had died in the first hour of Feng's gate alone.
But that was then. This was now. And now, Feng was just an arrogant young man with bad timing and worse instincts.

"I understand your concerns," Ren said, and his voice was calm—calm in a way that made the guests at nearby tables look up from their appetizers. Not the voice of a beaten son-in-law. The voice of someone who'd seen worse than anything this room could offer and walked away. "You think I have nothing to offer."
He stood.

Every guard in the room shifted. There were twelve of them—Ren counted without looking, the War God's tactical assessment running on instinct even in this powerless body. Four by the east doors, two flanking the patriarch's empty chair, six along the perimeter. All armed. All watching him.
"Sit *down*," Feng hissed.

Ren didn't. He looked at Liang Xue—not at her beauty, not at her coldness, but at the heirloom jade pendant at her throat. Green and ancient, warm against her skin, pulsing with an array signature so faint that no one in this room could detect it.
No one but him.
The Salvation System hummed at the edge of his consciousness.

`ARRAY DETECTED: Liang Bloodline Key Fragment`
`CLASSIFICATION: Portal Control Component`
`THREAT LEVEL: APOCALYPTIC`
`NOTE: Remove from proximity of subject and the firewall weakens. Keep on subject and it remains the single most dangerous object on this planet.`
*Wonderful,* Ren thought. The end of the world was hanging around his wife's neck, and she thought it was a family heirloom.
"I don't have money," Ren said quietly, still standing. "I don't have power. Not yet." He let the words settle. "But I'm not signing those papers tonight."
He walked away from the table—not toward the exit, but toward the banquet's open bar. Behind him, he could feel Liang Xue's gaze on his back, burning with something that wasn't quite anger.
At the bar, Ren ordered whiskey. Neat. The bartender's hands trembled slightly—everyone had seen the scene. The whole room had.
The Salvation System's countdown pulsed in his peripheral vision:
`COUNTDOWN: 1095 days, 14 hours, 23 minutes`
One thousand and ninety-five days until the Liang family's dimensional array reached completion and the Patriarch tore open the gates of hell.
One thousand and ninety-five days to stop it.
And he had 0.01% of his War God power, a mortal body, and a wife who wanted him gone.
*Shen Wei,* he thought at the system, *if you can hear me—I'm going to need a better plan than "don't get divorced."*
The system flickered.
`— always do`
Two words. Barely a whisper. But they were *her* cadence—the way she used to sign off on mission briefs, half-dismissive and half-tender.
Ren's grip on the whiskey glass tightened. Then he drank.
It was going to be a long three years.
